text.title: Solo Context-Switch Protocol — Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every solo founder day must enforce. These are HARD rules — fascinating Twitter, urgent-seeming DMs, and curiosity cannot override them.

rule.statement: The day MUST run three named modes — Builder, Operator, Inbox — in fixed order. Builder ALWAYS comes first; Inbox NEVER comes before Builder.

rationale.source: Cal Newport Deep Work + Mark/Gloria Cost of Interrupted Work research (CHI 2008)

rule.rationale: Inbox-first wrecks the morning attentional window; the order is the entire mechanism.

rule.statement: Inside a Builder block, ALL notification channels MUST be silenced at OS level: no Slack badge, no email pull, no Stripe SMS, no Twitter glance. Phone is in another room or face-down on do-not-disturb.

rationale.source: Mark et al., Switch! Reading Email and IM — recovery time = 23 min per interruption

rule.rationale: A single notification check inside a Builder block costs ~23 minutes of recovery; the silence rule is the cheapest possible protection.

rule.statement: Each block MUST end with a 5-minute handoff note answering three questions: "what was open?", "what is the next decision?", "what must the next mode remember?". Block transitions without the note are violations.
rule.rationale: Re-loading mental state at the start of a block costs 10–20 minutes; the note costs 5 and recovers the rest.

rule.statement: "Urgent" items discovered inside a Builder block MUST be written to the next Operator block's inbox; they cannot trigger an inline switch unless they fit the documented incident-override list (P0 outage, charge dispute, security incident).
rule.rationale: Most "urgent" customer messages are not urgent on a 4-hour latency; an explicit override list keeps the founder honest.

rule.statement: The protocol MUST be reviewed weekly: count of block violations + count of urgency-override invocations. A trend of ≥3 violations/week for 2 consecutive weeks triggers a structural fix (lengthen Operator block, hire support, scope cut), not a willpower attempt.

rationale.source: Systems-thinking discipline (Deming / Senge) — behaviour problems are usually system problems

rule.rationale: Repeat violations are signals that the block sizing is wrong, not that the founder is weak.

example.title: Anchored day
example.code: date: 2026-05-20 blocks: - mode: builder window: 09:00-12:00 notifications: silenced handoff_note: | Open: checkout-retry logic, branch fix/retry-422. Next decision: choose exponential vs fixed backoff (lean exp). Operator must: remember new env var STRIPE_RETRY_MAX=5. - mode: operator window: 13:00-15:00 handoff_note: | Open: 2 customer DMs replied; refund #482 issued. Next decision: write up the data-export FAQ (parked). Builder must: nothing pending. - mode: inbox window: 15:00-16:00 violations_today: 0
example.note: Order respected, notifications off, handoff notes present, no violations.

example.title: Un-anchored day
example.code: "Woke up, checked Twitter, replied to two DMs, opened email, saw a bug report, tried to code but kept checking Slack. Half-finished by 7pm."
example.note: Inbox-first, no silence, no handoff, no review. The classic solo-founder day-pattern that produces 8 hours of motion and 90 minutes of progress.
